CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The summary indicates a well-organized paper with a clear logical flow from method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (novel semi-automatic framework and DBPNLG). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and well-defined (ILP, property-bigram models, interpolation) and figures/tables (datasets, metrics) are reported as effective; overall writing quality is described as good. Minor presentation issues remain: some procedural details of the crowdsourcing QA (e.g., property renaming clarifications, the 8.7% corpus loss) could be presented more systematically to aid reproducibility, and explicit mention of released code/data is absent from the summary. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (novel semi-automatic framework and the DBPNLG corpus). Notation appears consistent and the methodological pipeline (KB extraction, property bigram models, ILP, crowdsourcing) is described clearly; figures/tables/examples supporting dataset statistics and comparisons (e.g., diversity metrics, perplexity/BLEU results) are effective. Writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity, though a few presentation details (e.g., clearer discussion of crowdsourcing losses and property renaming edge cases) could be expanded for complete reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The summary indicates a well-organized paper with a clear logical flow from method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (novel semi-automatic framework and DBPNLG). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and well-defined (ILP, property-bigram models, interpolation) and figures/tables (datasets, metrics) are reported as effective; overall writing quality is described as good. Minor presentation issues remain: some procedural details of the crowdsourcing QA (e.g., property renaming clarifications, the 8.7% corpus loss) could be presented more systematically to aid reproducibility, and explicit mention of released code/data is absent from the summary.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is overall well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (novel semi-automatic framework and the DBPNLG corpus). Notation appears consistent and the methodological pipeline (KB extraction, property bigram models, ILP, crowdsourcing) is described clearly; figures/tables/examples supporting dataset statistics and comparisons (e.g., diversity metrics, perplexity/BLEU results) are effective. Writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity, though a few presentation details (e.g., clearer discussion of crowdsourcing losses and property renaming edge cases) could be expanded for complete reproducibility.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents a useful, well-engineered pipeline that combines established components to produce a novel and challenging dataset (DBPNLG), making a clear applied contribution for dataset builders. However, its novelty is primarily in the integration and empirical validation rather than in a fundamentally new theoretical or algorithmic advance, so a moderate originality score is warranted. The score reflects the practical usefulness and thoughtful design while acknowledging the work is largely an incremental, application-level contribution rather than a conceptual breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper assembles a semi-automatic pipeline (KB subgraph extraction, property-bigram models, ILP-based content selection, and crowdsourced verbalization) to create DBPNLG, which is useful but largely composes established components rather than introducing a fundamentally new algorithmic paradigm. The formulation of interpolated property bigrams and an ILP for varied triple set selection is a non-trivial engineering design, but it lacks provable guarantees or a novel theoretical framing that would mark a major methodological advance. The authors do clearly differentiate their resource from crowdsourced RNNLG and provide empirical evidence that DBPNLG is more linguistically diverse and challenging, yet the contribution reads as an informed, incremental advance in dataset construction rather than a highly original conceptual breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a notable and practically useful pipeline for creating data-to-text micro-planning corpora from KBs—combining KB category graphs, learned property bigram models, and an ILP-based content selection step—which is a clear, novel application-level contribution beyond prior crowdsourced datasets. While the individual components (KB extraction, n‑gram modeling, ILP, crowdsourcing) are established, their integration to produce DBPNLG and the empirical evidence that it yields more linguistically/semantically diverse and challenging data than RNNLG provides meaningful differentiation. This would inform practitioners and dataset builders, though it is more an applied/engineering advance than a fundamentally new theoretical method.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical bottleneck and delivers a concrete, more diverse and challenging DBPNLG corpus that will be useful to the NLG micro-planning community. I give a 4 because the dataset and semi-automatic pipeline are a strong, actionable contribution likely to be adopted or extended, while remaining mindful of the valid concerns about limited theoretical framing, missing extrinsic evaluations, and incomplete reporting/code release that constrain its immediate, field-changing impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical bottleneck—automating creation of data-to-text micro-planning corpora—and produces a concrete resource (DBPNLG) with analyses showing greater semantic and linguistic diversity and higher modelling difficulty than RNNLG. However, from a formalist perspective its impact is muted by limited theoretical framing or provable guarantees for core design choices (e.g., interpolated property bigrams, ILP formulation), lack of downstream/extrinsic evaluations showing utility beyond intrinsic metrics, and incomplete reporting on annotation QA and reproducibility (no code/data release mentioned). These factors make the contribution a useful, moderate-impact resource that should prompt follow-up work rather than a field-changing advance.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical bottleneck—scalable creation of data-to-text training corpora—by proposing a semi-automatic KB-driven pipeline and producing DBPNLG from DBpedia, which is directly relevant to NLG micro-planning. The authors show DBPNLG is more semantically and linguistically diverse than RNNLG and that a standard seq2seq model performs worse (higher perplexity, lower BLEU), providing strong evidence the corpus is a more challenging and therefore useful benchmark. The crowd-sourcing + ILP/content-selection pipeline and the presented diversity analyses make this readily actionable and likely to be adopted or extended by others, though lack of explicit code release (not mentioned) and current coverage limited to six categories somewhat constrain immediate broad uptake. Overall, this is a strong, dataset-driven contribution that opens useful follow-up work.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a useful empirical contribution by creating and analyzing DBPNLG and demonstrating it is more linguistically challenging than RNNLG, and they both note clear limitations in ablation/sensitivity analysis, extrinsic evaluation, and reproducibility/annotation detail. Given the solid empirical work, thorough comparisons, and candid discussion of limitations, but acknowledging the important methodological gaps that prevent a top score, a 4/5 reflects strong contribution with room for improved validation and release practices.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a plausible semi-automatic pipeline instantiation on DBpedia and a range of intrinsic corpus analyses (shape diversity, CTTR, LS, perplexity, BLEU) supporting the claim that DBPNLG is linguistically and semantically more challenging than RNNLG. However, from a formalist perspective critical gaps remain: there are no formal guarantees or principled justification for key modeling choices (e.g., interpolated property bigrams vs. alternatives), ablation/sensitivity studies of those choices are not reported, and no downstream/extrinsic evaluation demonstrates utility of the corpus beyond intrinsic difficulty metrics. Reproducibility is weakly supported in the summary (code release not mentioned) and annotation QA lacks detailed worker qualifications and inter-annotator agreement statistics (only an overall 8.7% corpus loss is given).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a solid empirical evaluation: it implements the semi-automatic pipeline on DBpedia, releases DBPNLG (6 categories) and compares it to RNNLG using many linguistically-motivated metrics and a standard seq2seq baseline, showing DBPNLG is indeed more diverse and harder (higher perplexity, lower BLEU). Related work is reported as thorough, and the authors candidly discuss limitations (limited categories so far, smaller size than RNNLG, crowdsourcing losses and manual property clarifications). Where the work is slightly weak is in ablation/sensitivity analysis and full reproducibility — hyperparameters are reported but code/dataset release is not mentioned and it’s unclear how extensively the authors ablate the ILP/content-selection variants — hence not a perfect score.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a fair, controlled comparison to RNNLG using the same seq2seq baseline and standard metrics, and that the authors transparently report limitations. However, the absence of stronger micro-planning or structured NLG baselines, downstream/extrinsic evaluations, ablations, and no reported code release meaningfully weakens comparative claims; these gaps justify a cautious score rather than a higher one.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper conducts sensible direct comparisons to an existing crowdsourced benchmark (RNNLG) and evaluates an out-of-the-box seq2seq model on both corpora using the same metrics (perplexity, BLEU, lexical/diversity measures), so surface-level fairness is respected. However, from a theory-first evaluation perspective the comparison set is weak: no contemporary micro-planning or structured NLG baselines (beyond a generic seq2seq) and no downstream extrinsic tasks or ablations that would demonstrate practical utility, and code release is not reported—shortcomings that limit the paper's comparative claims. The authors do note that RNNLG yields higher model scores (i.e., is an easier benchmark) and analyze differences in input shape and lexical diversity, but the absence of stronger and more task-relevant baselines and reproducibility artifacts prevents a more favorable rating.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a reasonable and recent benchmark (RNNLG) and uses a standard sequence-to-sequence baseline with attention to evaluate learnability, reporting comparable evaluation metrics (BLEU, perplexity) and hyperparameters. Comparisons appear fair and controlled (same model applied to both corpora) and the authors honestly acknowledge when RNNLG is larger and note dataset limitations. While the paper discusses related work thoroughly, it relies on a single off-the-shelf neural baseline rather than a wider set of contemporary data-to-text models, which is a minor omission but does not undermine the overall fairness and usefulness of the comparisons.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper targets the right NLP/ML audience and makes a valuable empirical/data contribution by producing a carefully engineered micro-planning corpus and providing analyses showing it challenges seq2seq models. I rate it a 4 because, while the dataset and evaluations are useful and well-executed (as Reviewer B notes), Reviewer A’s concerns about limited theoretical guarantees, lack of downstream/extrinsic validation, sparse ablations, and unclear code/data release reduce its suitability for a top-tier acceptance without revisions to address reproducibility and practical utility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a topic squarely within NLP/ML venues (data-to-text micro-planning) and targets the right audience by proposing a semi-automatic corpus creation pipeline and empirical comparisons to RNNLG. However, from a formalist perspective it leans heavily on dataset construction and empirical diagnostics without provable guarantees for its core ILP/bigram design choices, and it only evaluates impact via an off-the-shelf seq2seq baseline rather than any downstream extrinsic task or ablation demonstrating practical utility. Annotation QA is only superficially reported (majority vote, 8.7% discard) and the absence of a stated code/data release further weakens reproducibility — together these factors make it a borderline fit for a top-tier conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP venue: it introduces a carefully engineered, reproducible pipeline for creating a data-to-text micro-planning corpus (DBPNLG) from DBpedia, includes rigorous ILP-based content selection, crowdsourced verbalizations with majority-vote validation and quality checks, and compares against RNNLG using appropriate linguistic and model-based metrics (perplexity, BLEU, CTTR, LS). From a data-centric perspective I value that the authors demonstrate DBPNLG is more challenging for standard seq2seq models and provide detailed analyses of input-shape and lexical diversity; these are the kinds of resources that drive empirical progress. The admitted limitations (limited categories, smaller size than RNNLG, some annotation loss) are transparently reported and do not undermine venue relevance. Overall the work targets the right audience and uses evaluation standards expected at top NLP conferences.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical claims are supported by comparative corpus statistics and modeling results, and that the ILP/content-selection and crowdsourcing pipeline are plausible and executed. Remaining concerns (limited theoretical characterization, incomplete annotation QA details, narrow category coverage, single model family, and lack of released code/statistical testing) are substantive but do not invalidate the main findings, so a score of 4 reflects that the work is largely sound with some important but remediable limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's core claims — a semi-automatic KB-to-text pipeline producing a more linguistically diverse DBPNLG and that a standard seq2seq model finds it harder than RNNLG — are supported by comparative corpus statistics and model perplexity/BLEU results. However, from a theory-first standpoint the work lacks formal characterization or guarantees for the ILP/content-selection and no principled justification is given for the interpolated bigram design versus alternatives; there are also missing annotation QA details (worker qualifications, inter-annotator agreement and disagreement analysis) and no downstream/extrinsic evaluation to demonstrate practical utility. Experimental methodology is reasonable (comparative metrics and an off-the-shelf baseline) but limited by using a single model family, incomplete ablations of design choices, and no released code noted for reproducibility, so the evidence is suggestive but not fully conclusive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s central claims are clearly articulated and supported by a reasonable set of experiments showing DBPNLG is more diverse and harder for a standard seq2seq model (higher perplexity, lower BLEU) than RNNLG, and the crowdsourcing + validation pipeline and ILP-based content selection are described and empirically exercised. The work appears technically sound — the ILP/content-selection and property bigram modeling are plausible and the human-verification steps with an 8.7% corpus loss show honest QC — but some practical gaps remain (only 6 DBpedia categories currently evaluated, DBPNLG is smaller than RNNLG, and no mention of released code or statistical significance testing). Given these are minor-to-moderate limitations that do not overturn the empirical conclusions, I score it a 4.

main_claims: Proposes a novel semi-automatic framework to create data-to-text training corpora for NLG micro-planning from existing knowledge bases (KBs).
Implements the framework on DBpedia to produce DBPNLG and shows DBPNLG is more semantically and linguistically diverse than the crowdsourced RNNLG dataset despite being smaller.

Demonstrates that a standard sequence-to-sequence neural model achieves lower performance (higher perplexity and lower BLEU) on DBPNLG than on RNNLG, indicating DBPNLG is a more challenging benchmark for learning micro-planning.
_summary.methodology: Extract category-specific entity graphs from a KB (DBpedia), learn property bigram models (sibling, chain, interpolated), formulate content selection as an ILP to produce varied RDF triple sets, then crowdsource human verbalisations for single triples and merged multi-triple inputs with automatic and manual quality checks and majority-vote validation.
datasets: DBPNLG (created in this work from DBpedia)
baselines: Sequence-to-sequence model with attention (Vinyals et al., 2015) used as an out-of-the-box comparison
RNNLG dataset as a comparative benchmark
limitations: Current reported DBPNLG experiments cover only 6 DBpedia categories (authors note content for 14 more will be collected).
DBPNLG is smaller in size than the RNNLG dataset (RNNLG is reported as more than twice larger).
Crowdsourcing requires manual clarification of unclear DBpedia properties (e.g., property renaming) to obtain usable verbalisations.
Crowdsourced verification led to an overall corpus loss of 8.7% (i.e., some collected verbalizations were discarded).

reviewer_a.name: Formalist Theorist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a track record of theoretical models for NLG and formal evaluation methodologies.
traits: Penalises papers that primarily present new datasets or empirical benchmarks without a formal theoretical framing or provable guarantees for their core algorithmic components (e.g., ILP formulation or property bigrams).

Reduces score if the paper lacks rigorous extrinsic/ downstream evaluations demonstrating the dataset's utility beyond intrinsic metrics (expects at least one downstream task or ablation showing practical impact).
Demands detailed annotation QA statistics (worker qualification, inter-annotator agreement, disagreement analysis); deducts points when these are missing or superficial.
Penalises absence of thorough ablation studies or formal comparisons for design choices (e.g., why interpolated bigrams vs. other models), and expects public release of code/data for reproducibility.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critical, theory-first reviewer who privileges formal arguments, principled ablations, and thorough evaluative evidence over empirical novelty alone.

reviewer_b.name: Pragmatic Dataset Champion
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist specializing in data-centric NLP and crowd-annotated benchmark construction, with multiple dataset releases and applied evaluation experience.
traits: Awards higher scores to papers that introduce new, well-documented datasets/benchmarks with transparent crowdsourcing pipelines and clear quality-control procedures.
Gives credit when authors provide evidence the dataset is more challenging for standard models (e.g., higher perplexity, lower BLEU) and treat this as a desirable property for driving progress.

Favors papers that include linguistic diversity analyses and sanity checks comparing to existing corpora, and rewards careful dataset composition methods (e.g., KB-driven generation, ILP-based selection) even if not theoretically formalized.
Increases score when authors commit to releasing data and scripts and when they validate annotations via majority vote and manual checks.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Practically oriented reviewer who values high-quality resources and reproducible engineering that push empirical progress, tolerating lower theoretical novelty when the dataset and validation are solid.
